title: Piperia dilatata
taxonomy: Piperia dilatata is classified within the family Orchidaceae. This genus and species falls within the subfamily Orchidoideae. The subfamily is further divided into tribes, and P. dilatata is a part of the tribe Neottieae. The tribe Neottieae is native to the Americas and contains around 60 species. P. dilatata is native to the Pacific coastal regions of North America.
uses: Piperia dilatata, also known as the white fringed orchid, has been used in traditional medicine by indigenous cultures in North America. Its medicinal properties include antiseptic and anti-inflammatory effects, which have been used to treat skin irritations and wounds. The plant contains compounds that have been shown to have antimicrobial and antioxidant properties, making it a potential treatment for various health conditions. Additionally, Piperia dilatata has been used in traditional medicine to treat respiratory issues, such as coughs and colds. However, further research is needed to confirm the plant's efficacy and potential side effects.

side_effects: Piperia dilatata, also known as the green lip orchid, is a plant that can be toxic if ingested. However, there is limited information available on its side effects. intro: Piperia dilatata, also known as the Pacific piperia, is a terrestrial orchid native to the Pacific Northwest region of North America. The roots of this plant have been traditionally used in herbal medicine, particularly for their potential to support cardiovascular health by lowering blood pressure and cholesterol levels. The bioactive constituents of Piperia dilatata include piperidine alkaloids, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made with Piperia dilatata are tinctures, teas, and capsules, often used to promote cardiovascular well-being and alleviate symptoms of anxiety and stress.
